behaviors.description: Required patient fields (name, DOB, insurance ID) are validated before any downstream processing — missing fields produce a clear error listing all missing items.
behaviors.justification: This is pure validation logic on local data. A real failure (missing field accepted, crashes downstream) is caught by a Unit test of the validation function with parametrized missing-field inputs. No I/O, no external dependencies, no concurrency.
behaviors.test_description: Call the field validator with combinations of missing fields (missing name, missing DOB, missing insurance ID, all missing). Assert the returned error message includes the name of each missing field and no field is skipped.

behaviors.description: Date of birth validation rejects future dates, dates more than 150 years ago, and non-date strings.
behaviors.justification: Date validation is a self-contained function with no I/O. Real failures (accepting an invalid date, applying the wrong comparison logic) are caught by Unit tests with parametrized boundary cases (tomorrow, today-150.5 years, Feb 30, '02/99/3000'). No external dependencies or infrastructure beyond the function itself.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized Unit test: pass (future_date, date_150_years_ago, date_151_years_ago, invalid_string_like_'99/99/9999'). Assert each is rejected with a clear error message indicating which constraint was violated.

behaviors.description: The insurance eligibility check sends the correct patient ID and plan code to the payer API and correctly interprets the 'eligible' vs 'not eligible' vs 'pending' response.
behaviors.justification: This behavior has two parts: (1) forming and sending the correct HTTP request with patient_id and plan_code, and (2) parsing the response. To catch a real failure like sending patient_id=null or misinterpreting response codes requires testing the integration between the intake module and the API client. Mocking the API is necessary, but validating the request payload and response handling requires wiring multiple modules together. Unit-level mocking might miss failures in how the request is constructed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the payer API client. Call intake with test patient data. Assert the mock was invoked with the correct patient_id and plan_code in the request payload. Call intake again with each response type (eligible, not eligible, pending) and assert the system correctly interprets each one into the intake's eligibility field.

behaviors.description: When the payer API is unreachable, the system marks eligibility as 'pending' and continues the intake rather than blocking.
behaviors.justification: Testing that a missing external dependency doesn't crash the system requires testing the error path of the API client integration. A real failure (code raises the exception instead of catching it, or doesn't set eligibility='pending') is caught by Integration testing with a mocked API that raises a connection error. The intake module must wire together error handling logic with the API client's exception contract.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the payer API client to raise a ConnectionError. Call intake. Assert no exception escapes intake, and the eligibility field is set to 'pending'. Assert downstream steps (scheduling, triage) proceed without additional errors.

behaviors.description: The AI symptom triage correctly assigns high urgency to symptom combinations that indicate emergency conditions (e.g., chest pain + shortness of breath).
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on what the real LLM produces for a given symptom combination. LLMs are non-deterministic; the same input might receive slightly different urgency assignments across runs. A Unit test with a mocked LLM that always returns 'high' would pass but miss real failures where the LLM occasionally returns 'medium' or an unrelated classification. Only calling the real medical reasoning LLM and observing its actual output can verify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real AI triage module with symptom input ('chest pain', 'shortness of breath'). Verify the returned urgency level includes 'high' or 'emergency'. Run multiple times (at least 5) to detect if the LLM sometimes assigns a lower urgency, indicating the model may not be suitable for this use case.

behaviors.description: The symptom triage produces a natural language explanation of its urgency assessment that a clinician can review, not just a numeric score.
behaviors.justification: Verifying that the explanation is natural language (not just a number like '8' or 'urgency_score=5') requires observing the real LLM output. A Unit test with a mocked LLM can verify the intake system passes the explanation through without stripping it, but cannot verify the REAL LLM produces natural language. The behavior asks the system to produce an explanation; only calling the real LLM confirms this.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real AI triage with multiple symptom combinations (chest pain, severe fever, mild cough, etc.). Assert each response includes an explanation field. Verify the explanation is not empty and contains prose-like text (not solely numeric codes or symptom lists).

behaviors.description: An appointment is scheduled in the first available slot that matches the assigned urgency level — high urgency gets same-day, medium gets within 48 hours.
behaviors.justification: Verifying the scheduling algorithm correctly queries the calendar DB and selects the first available matching slot requires testing against a real (test) database with realistic calendar state. A Unit test with a mocked calendar (e.g., in-memory list) might miss real failures like: the query doesn't handle the real DB schema, indexes don't work as expected, or timezone logic breaks with real timestamps. Integration testing with a test calendar DB ensures the query and slot-matching logic works against real schema.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a test calendar DB with appointments on different days and times. Call scheduling for a high-urgency patient and a medium-urgency patient. Assert high-urgency patient gets a same-day slot, medium-urgency gets a slot within 48 hours. Assert the selected slot is the earliest available matching the urgency constraint.

behaviors.description: The scheduling step does not double-book a time slot even when two patients are being processed concurrently.
behaviors.justification: A race condition (two threads both reading the same slot as available, both writing to it) cannot be caught by a single-threaded Unit test with a mocked calendar. Real failures manifest only under concurrent access. Integration testing with actual concurrent processes/threads and a real (test) calendar database can expose race conditions through transaction behavior and row-level locking.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two concurrent processes (or threads with proper synchronization points). Each calls intake with a different patient, both should be scheduled. Both should target the last available same-day slot. Assert that after both intakes complete, no time slot is double-booked; exactly two different slots are occupied.

behaviors.description: The confirmation message includes the patient name, appointment time, provider name, and preparation instructions appropriate to the visit type.
behaviors.justification: Verifying the message content is correctly assembled is a pure data transformation. A real failure (wrong provider name in message, missing instructions) is caught by a Unit test with mocked patient, appointment, and provider data, asserting the confirmation text includes all required fields. No I/O, no external dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Create mocked patient, appointment, and provider objects. Call the confirmation message formatter. Assert the returned message string includes: patient name (e.g., 'John Doe'), appointment time (e.g., '2026-03-28 10:00 AM'), provider name (e.g., 'Dr. Smith'), and appropriate prep instructions (e.g., 'Fast for 12 hours' for a lab visit).

behaviors.description: The full intake pipeline — validate, check insurance, triage symptoms, schedule, confirm — completes for a real patient submission without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end pipeline involving: (1) validation (Unit), (2) insurance API call (System/Integration), (3) LLM triage (Agentic, with non-deterministic output), (4) DB scheduling (Integration), and (5) confirmation assembly (Unit). A real failure could occur at any step or in the integration between steps. Testing only the happy path with all externals mocked (Integration) or with a mocked LLM (System) would miss failures that manifest under real LLM non-determinism or when real API/DB behavior surprises the pipeline. The pipeline includes at least one Agentic component (LLM triage), making this Agentic+Integration = Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full intake pipeline with real patient data and a real LLM triage engine (but test insurance API and calendar DB). Verify all steps complete without raising an exception: fields validate, insurance API returns a response (or system continues with 'pending'), LLM returns an urgency and explanation, an appointment slot is found and reserved, and a confirmation message is generated.
